serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of a chicken burrito bowl is approximately 400 g, representing a full restaurant-style bowl containing rice, grilled chicken, beans, vegetables, salsa, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Burrito Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Burrito Bowl (cr_id G00010836), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed rice-and-protein dishes analogous estimate; white rice GI reference (Atkinson et al., 2021)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken burrito bowl. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the substantial protein (10.91 g/100g), fat (13.71 g/100g), dietary fibre (1.4 g/100g), and legume content (black beans, GI ~30), which collectively lower the effective glycemic response. Composite dish GI estimated conservatively at 55. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (40.1 g for 400 g serving × 20.06/100) / 100 = 55 × 80.24/100 = 44.1 / 2 ≈ 22.1 (using standard GL formula: GI × available carb in serving / 100 = 55 × 40.1 / 100 = 22.1).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold with insufficient potassium offset (126 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish in standard portions; if consumed, request reduced-salt marinade and salsa, avoid added cheese and sour cream, and restrict serving size to no more than 200 g to keep sodium intake below 940 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (20.06 g/100g) with low sugar (1.75 g/100g); protein and fat slow glucose absorption but portion control is essential
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions (200–300 g); substituting brown rice for white rice will lower the glycemic index and increase fibre, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables will further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (212 mg/100g) and high sodium (470 mg/100g); saturated fat data unavailable but cheese and sour cream contribute saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the dish without cheese and sour cream to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol load, limit portion to 200–250 g to manage sodium intake, and choose guacamole as a heart-healthy monounsaturated fat alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomato-based salsa) may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; sodium 470 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are nightshade-sensitive should request the dish without bell peppers and tomato salsa, ask for reduced-sodium preparation, and add extra guacamole as a source of anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Burrito Bowl is a Mexican-American Tex-Mex composite dish consisting of grilled or seasoned chicken served over a base of white rice, accompanied by black beans, corn, sautéed bell peppers, fresh salsa, shredded cheese, sour cream, and guacamole, all assembled in a single bowl without a tortilla wrap. The dish is popular in fast-casual restaurant settings and is widely available in Singapore as an imported Western food concept. The chicken provides lean protein (10.91 g/100g) and the rice contributes the primary carbohydrate load (20.06 g/100g). Fat content is moderate-to-high at 13.71 g/100g, contributed by cheese, sour cream, and guacamole. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100g due to seasoned marinades, salsa, and cheese, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Cholesterol is notably high at 212 mg/100g. Sugar is low at 1.75 g/100g. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~55) due to the moderating effect of protein, fat, fibre, and legumes on the white rice base. It is suitable for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions with brown rice substitution. The dish is not suitable for hypertension without significant sodium reduction modifications. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients sensitive to nightshades without ingredient substitutions.
common_user_queries: Is chicken burrito bowl good for diabetes?
Is chicken burrito bowl high in sodium?
Is burrito bowl suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Chicken Burrito Bowl (cr_id G00010836) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database reference data for white rice and legumes, clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010836 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source record for this dish. All -1 values reflect genuine data absence in the approved source, not estimation gaps. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI reference data from the University of Sydney GI Database moderated by protein, fat, fibre, and legume content; no direct GI measurement for chicken burrito bowl was found in any approved GI source. Cholesterol at 212 mg/100g is notably high for a chicken-based dish and may reflect the combined contribution of chicken, cheese, and sour cream in the HPB-tested formulation. This dish is classified as Western/Tex-Mex cuisine and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual compliance rule, which applies to Singaporean dishes only.
